FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study" with Year 2 and Year 4 follow-ups; U.S. cohort confirmed

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective/longitudinal empirical analysis testing associations between social media use, sleep duration, and brain activation over two years

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Sleep Research; no preprint or duplicate indicators

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (self-reported, Youth Screen Time Survey) measured as IV at Year 2; sleep duration (Munich Chronotype Questionnaire) as DV at Year 4; brain activation in reward/executive control regions (neural variable) as moderator; individual-trait-level outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported: prospective associations with moderation by brain activation; e.g., "Greater social media use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep duration at Year 4, particularly among adolescents with lower activation in the nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula and putamen"

FT_IC4.evidence: Moderation effects quantified; prospective model includes regression-style estimates and conditional effects by neural activation levels

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional design: SM is IV predicting sleep at Year 4, AND sleep is IV predicting SM use at later wave. EC7 does not apply when SM appears on both sides of the equation
notes: No PDF available; all coding from abstract. Strong prospective design with neural moderation. Clear individual-trait-level outcome (sleep duration, a behavioral variable).

FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study" with N=11,878 children ages 9-10 at baseline; U.S. ABCD cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data examining prevalence and correlates of emotion dysregulation (ED) measured via Child Behavior Checklist subscales

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology; no preprint/duplicate indicators

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions "screen time use" as one of many correlates examined alongside demographic (age, sex, parental education, household income), contextual (neighborhood safety), and clinical variables (psychiatric disorders, sleep disturbance). No analysis pairs a qualifying smartphone/SM variable with an individual-trait-level outcome. Screen time is not analyzed as IV or DV in a SM↔trait relationship; it is listed only as a measured variable among many correlates of ED. No SM-outcome pairing documented.

notes: No PDF available. FT-IC2 fails because the paper does not analyze a qualifying SM/phone variable paired with an individual-trait-level outcome. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive DevelopmentSM (ABCD®) Study" with data from years 2, 3, and 4; N≈7,260 participants; U.S. ABCD cohort confirmed

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models and parallel mediation model examining longitudinal association between ADHD diagnosis and gaming addiction symptoms (VGAQ) with psychosocial mediators

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Frontiers in Psychiatry (March 2026); no preprint or duplicate indicators

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper analyzes gaming addiction (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, VGAQ) as DV, with ADHD diagnosis as IV. Gaming/video games are explicitly excluded from the scope (§2.4: "console/PC-gaming-only" does not qualify). The VGAQ is a generic gaming addiction measure with no device-specific breakdown isolating smartphone-based gaming from console/PC gaming. Gaming does not qualify as a smartphone or social media use variable; no SM/phone-specific operationalization is present.

notes: Full PDF reviewed. FT-IC2 fails because gaming addiction (generic, not smartphone/SM-isolated) is not a qualifying exposure variable. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.
